lineitem.notes: Largest table in TPC-H, accessed in all 3 queries. Heavy aggregation workload. Date range filters dominant.

customer.notes: Filtered by c_mktsegment in Q3, then joined to orders

orders.notes: Bridge between customer and lineitem in Q3

nation.notes: Not accessed in sample queries but part of schema

region.notes: Not accessed in sample queries but part of schema

supplier.notes: Not accessed in sample queries but part of schema

part.notes: Not accessed in sample queries but part of schema

partsupp.notes: Not accessed in sample queries but part of schema

joins.notes: Q3: customer filtered by mktsegment first (highly selective), then joined to orders
Q3: orders filtered by date, then joined to lineitem (also filtered by date)

filters.notes: Date cutoff filter, likely selects ~98% of rows (all but last 90 days)
Narrow range [0.05, 0.07], very selective (~2% of rows)
l_quantity < 24, moderately selective (~50% assuming uniform distribution)
Post-date filter in Q3, combined with other filters
Equality on market segment, highly selective (~20% assuming 5 segments)
Pre-date filter, combined with customer filter

aggregations.notes: Very low cardinality grouping (2x2 combinations). Aggregates 6 different metrics. Perfect for sorted or direct-mapped aggregation.
Medium-high cardinality grouping. l_orderkey is nearly unique after filters. Requires hash aggregation.

ordering.notes: Top-K query (LIMIT 10). Can use heap-based partial sort instead of full sort. Sort on computed revenue column.

optimization_opportunities: Q1: Full lineitem scan - CRITICAL for parallelism (6M rows). Date filter pushdown, columnar access for hot columns only (8 of 16 columns)
Q1: Very low cardinality aggregation (4 groups) - use direct-mapped array instead of hash table
Q1: Computed columns in aggregation (l_extendedprice * (1-l_discount) * (1+l_tax)) - consider operator fusion to avoid materialization
Q3: Filter pushdown - apply c_mktsegment='BUILDING' before join (reduces customer by ~80%)
Q3: Filter pushdown - apply date filters before join (both o_orderdate and l_shipdate)
Q3: Join ordering - customer→orders→lineitem is optimal (smallest to largest after filters)
Q3: Hash join with bloom filter - build on filtered customer, probe with orders. Use bloom filter on o_custkey to reduce orders scan
Q3: Top-K optimization - use min-heap for LIMIT 10 instead of full sort
Q3: Semi-join reduction - customer filter is very selective, propagate to reduce orders/lineitem scans
Q6: Multiple conjunctive filters - evaluate cheapest filter first (l_quantity < 24 is integer comparison, do before discount range check)
Q6: Single aggregation result - can use SIMD for entire pipeline (filter + compute revenue)
All queries: Columnar storage for lineitem (analytical workload, selective column access)
All queries: Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality string columns (l_returnflag, l_linestatus, c_mktsegment)
All queries: Zone maps on date columns (l_shipdate, o_orderdate) for block-level pruning
Cross-query: l_shipdate accessed in all 3 queries - prime candidate for sorted storage or clustered indexing

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: Largest table, accessed in all queries. Morsel-driven parallel scan (10K-100K rows per morsel). Expected speedup: 7-8x on 8 cores.
Q1: Partial aggregation per thread (local agg on 4 groups), then merge. Near-linear speedup expected.
Q3: Partition lineitem by l_orderkey hash, parallel probe against orders hash table. Expected speedup: 6-7x (accounting for hash table build overhead).
Q3: Parallel scan with date filter. Expected speedup: 5-6x on 8 cores.
Q3: Small enough that parallel scan may not be beneficial (overhead > benefit). Consider serial scan with filter, or parallel only if filter is expensive.

column_co_access_patterns.notes: Always accessed together for revenue computation (l_extendedprice * (1-l_discount)). Store adjacently in columnar layout.
Q1 groups by both. Low cardinality - consider combined dictionary encoding.
Q6 accesses these 4 columns only. Excellent columnar storage case - skip other 12 columns.
Q3 projects both for final result. Store adjacently.

critical_path_analysis.rationale: Multi-way join (3 tables) with large intermediate results. Lineitem is 6M rows, orders is 1.5M rows. Even after filters, join likely produces >1M intermediate tuples. Q1 and Q6 are simpler single-table scans.

estimated_relative_cost.Q3: 3-5x (3-way join + date filters + medium-cardinality aggregation + sort)
estimated_relative_cost.Q6: 0.3x (single lineitem scan with highly selective filters, single result)

optimization_priority: Q3: Join optimization (ordering, hash join, bloom filters, parallelism)

lineitem.l_shipdate: Likely uniformly distributed over 7-year period (1992-1998 in TPC-H). Range filters very effective.
lineitem.l_discount: Typically discrete values (0.00, 0.01, ..., 0.10). BETWEEN 0.05-0.07 is ~20-30% of domain but may be less in practice.
lineitem.l_returnflag: Low cardinality (A, N, R). Perfect for dictionary encoding.
lineitem.l_linestatus: Low cardinality (O, F). Perfect for dictionary encoding.

customer.c_mktsegment: 5 segments in TPC-H (AUTOMOBILE, BUILDING, FURNITURE, MACHINERY, HOUSEHOLD). c_mktsegment = 'BUILDING' is ~20% selectivity.

orders.o_orderdate: Date range similar to l_shipdate. o_orderdate < '1995-03-15' is ~50% selectivity (mid-range cutoff).

summary: TPC-H workload is dominated by lineitem table (6M rows, fact table) with heavy analytical aggregation. Q1 and Q6 are single-table scans with filters and aggregations - excellent parallelism candidates. Q3 is a 3-way join (customer→orders→lineitem) with selective filters - requires careful join ordering and filter pushdown. Key optimization opportunities: (1) Parallel scans on lineitem (all queries), (2) Columnar storage (selective column access, 8/16 columns in Q1, 4/16 in Q6), (3) Filter pushdown in Q3 (mktsegment and date filters reduce data by 80-90% before join), (4) Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality strings, (5) Top-K heap for Q3 LIMIT 10, (6) Direct-mapped aggregation for Q1 (only 4 groups), (7) SIMD for Q6 filters and computation. Expected speedups: 7-8x from parallelism, 2-3x from columnar layout, 2-5x from hash table optimizations. Q3 is the bottleneck query (3-5x cost of Q1) and should be optimization priority.
